mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers the nature, varieties, and scope of logic, including necessity, decision theory, and descriptions, central to both A (history) and B (philosophy of logic).
Aristotle founded the logic of predicates, developed syllogistic and modal logic, and is the pivotal figure in ancient logic covered in A.1.b.
Plato is cited as a precursor of ancient logic in A.1.a, contributing theories of language and the axiomatic method.
Socrates is identified as a precursor of ancient logic in A.1.a, contributing to theories of language and argumentation.
The Presocratics including Parmenides and Democritus contributed early reasoning and argumentation that preceded formal logic in A.1.
The Stoics founded the logic of propositions (A.1.d), a fundamental branch of formal logic.
Covers the philosophical context of ancient logic including the Sophists, Plato, Aristotle, and Hellenistic schools central to A.1.
Medieval logic is a major topic (A.2), covering Arabic contributions, William of Ockham, the old and new logic, and medieval theories of language and supposition.
A.2.a specifically mentions Arabic contributions to medieval logic, making Islamic philosophical traditions directly relevant.
Avicenna's contributions to Islamic philosophy included significant work in logic, relevant to medieval logic developments in A.2.a.
Aquinas engaged extensively with Aristotelian logic in his Scholastic method, relevant to medieval logic summations in A.2.
Descartes's method and discourse influenced logic in the Renaissance (A.3.a), including the Port-Royal logic.
Leibniz is cited in A.3.b for his characteristica universalis and logical calculus, key contributions to mathematical logic during the Enlightenment.
Kant's critical philosophy deeply engaged with logic; his deduction and rationalism relate to Enlightenment-era logic developments in A.3.b-c.
Russell's Logicism and theory of types are explicitly discussed in A.4.a as a major 20th-century development in logic.
Wittgenstein made foundational contributions to 20th-century logic and the philosophy of logic, including work on logical semantics (A.4, B).
Godel's incompleteness theorems are specifically cited in B.1.b as a central problem of logic, covering consistency and axiomatization.
Analytic philosophy was deeply intertwined with 20th-century developments in formal logic, including Frege and Russell, relevant to A.4 and B.
A.5.a covers Indian logic, its origins in scriptural commentaries (sutras), special problems in grammar, and types of inference.
A.5.b discusses Chinese logic originating in controversies between Confucianism, Taoism, and Moism, and its neglect after Neo-Confucianism.
Covers abstraction and Euclidean methods relevant to the rise of mathematical logic and the relation between logic and foundations of mathematics (A.3.c).

mappings.vsiAuthor: Emma Borg and Sarah A. Fisher
mappings.rationaleAI: Directly addresses formal semantics, reference, and truth -- core topics in logical semantics and problems of meaning discussed in B.2.a.
B.2.b addresses problems of ontology including individuation and existence, which are core metaphysical concerns in the philosophy of logic.
Covers the historical development of mathematics including its relationship to logic, relevant to A.3.c on Boole, Frege, and Cantor.
